frictions.text: Reduction theorems in this domain (e.g. 'JC in general reduces to the cubic-homogeneous case', 'suffices to further restrict to the Druzkowski cubic-linear form') are logical equivalences: the general Jacobian Conjecture holds iff it holds on the reduced class, and a counterexample found in the reduced class would equally refute the general claim. draw-ground only expresses one-directional partial support (X is a reason for Y), so I could only encode the 'suffices' direction (reduced-class-node -> general-class-node, n005->n004, n004->n001), which understates that these are exact/lossless equivalences rather than ordinary inductive/partial support like the proved special cases (Wang deg<=2, Moh n=2) sitting on the same kind of edge into n001. The grammar has no way to mark an edge as 'this reduction is an iff' versus 'this is one line of partial evidence among several'.

Boundary/necessity facts (n008: the char-p analog x-x^p fails; n009: the real non-vanishing-Jacobian analog fails per Pinchuk) state which hypotheses of the Jacobian Conjecture are essential -- they show a nearby, weaker statement is false, which is content about the theorem's exact hypotheses, not evidence for-or-against the root claim n001/n002 itself. Grounding them toward n001 would misrepresent them as bearing on JC's truth value; grounding them toward n002 would misrepresent 'a related statement fails' as 'JC itself has a counterexample'. The grammar's Ground/Antithesis vocabulary has no relation for 'this delineates why the theorem's hypotheses can't be weakened, adjacent to but not evidentially about the root question' -- so these nodes are left deliberately ungrounded at the top level, seated for future lane-5 (boundary analysis) structure to attach beneath them instead.

The certificate formats in BRIEF-jacobian.md sec6 (identity/invertibility/non-injectivity/exhaustion) are defined for a 'polynomial map F'. To isolate the general real-analytic mechanism behind Pinchuk's boundary fact (nonvanishing Jacobian != properness), the cleanest illustration is a smooth but non-polynomial map (e.g. the complex-exponential-as-real-map (e^x cos y, e^x sin y), chk-l5-03). It verifies cleanly as identity+non-injectivity, but there is no certificate category for 'illustrative non-polynomial construction used to isolate a mechanism that a polynomial counterexample also exploits' -- I reused the identity/non-injectivity semantics loosely rather than being told to.

Grounds/Dependents conflates two different inferential relations I needed this round: (1) ordinary evidential support ('X is a reason to believe Y'), and (2) mechanistic explanation of an ALREADY-established fact ('X is why Y is possible/true', e.g. n024 grounds n008, n026 grounds n009 -- n008/n009 are established boundary facts from the brief, not claims needing evidential support). Both render as the same Ground edge with no way to mark that the second kind presupposes rather than argues for its dependent. propose-typing could label individual edges this way, but there's no first-class 'explains' relation distinct from 'supports', so a reader can't tell the two apart without reading both texts.

The VERIFY-CONVENTION machine-checked-anchor mechanism only recognizes concrete computational constructions (a sympy identity/invertibility/non-injectivity/exhaustion instance) as exempt from routine rating. General mathematical theorems I stated this round (n016: local biholomorphism from the holomorphic inverse function theorem; n017: the covering-space criterion, a standard theorem in differential topology; n021: Hilbert's Nullstellensatz applied to nonvanishing polynomials over an algebraically closed field) are just as rigorously settled and non-contestable to a working mathematician as a CAS-checked arithmetic identity -- but because they are general theorems rather than 'a computation run on a specific F', there is no certificate format for them under sec6, so they fall into the ordinary blind-rating pool alongside genuinely open/contested claims like n001/n002. The grammar conflates 'rigorously settled but not machine-checkable as a single instance' with 'openly contested' -- both just get an A rating with no anchor flag.

Node n044 states an equivalence (Keller map is an automorphism IFF it is proper). One direction is a one-line triviality (automorphism => proper, from continuity of the inverse); the other carries all the substantive content (proper => automorphism, via finite-etale-covering-of-simply-connected-space). The grammar has no way to record that a node's two logical halves have wildly different depth/certainty -- splitting into two separate implication-claims would create two nodes with no Ground or Antithesis relation between them (they are not rivals, and neither is 'evidence for' the other in the support sense: they are two co-equal halves of one iff). I kept it as one node and lost that asymmetry.

Grounding a verified-computation evidence node into a universally-quantified claim (e.g. n047 -> n038, 'for ANY Keller map F with deg P+deg Q>2...') carries a quantifier mismatch the Ground edge cannot express: the evidence establishes the claim for ONE concrete instance, not the universal statement. A single CAS check and a would-be exhaustive proof both render as the same 'X grounds Y' edge with no way to mark 'this is one confirming data point among infinitely many needed' versus 'this instance-check is actually a full proof because the general argument specializes trivially.' The existing skeleton has the same pattern (n012->n003, n013->n005); I inherited rather than solved it, and it seems structural to how this graph mixes CAS-verified instances with universally-quantified claims -- Edge Agreement (ASSESSMENT-SPEC) partly compensates by letting a rater discount the inferential step, but there's no dedicated relation for 'exemplar of' as distinct from 'entails'.

Drawing n052 (general closed-form: rank-1 nilpotent 2x2 Druzkowski map A=uv^T is Keller iff a*c*(a-b)*(a+b)=0) as a Ground into n036 (the specific 24/32 exhaustion count over one finite matrix family) forced an ordinary support edge to carry a different relation: 'this general theorem specializes to/entails that finite-family count' is a deductive-containment relation, not evidential support in the usual sense (n036's truth is not independent evidence for n052; it is a corollary of it). The grammar's single undifferentiated Ground relation reads identically whether the edge means 'this is evidence for that' or 'that is a special case that follows from this' -- a reader can't tell from the edge alone which relation is meant, only by reading both node texts.

Ground/Dependent has one edge meaning (from is a Ground of to = support), but the brief's round-2 mandate asked for 'constraint claims that ground INTO n002' -- these are necessary-condition-if-n002-holds claims (e.g. n056: deg>=3), not evidence FOR n002's truth. I drew the edges constraint_claim -> n002 anyway (matching n044->n045's unused precedent) since it's the only mechanically available direction, but the polarity is backwards from what draw-ground normally signals (support), and a reader could misread these as advocacy. No clean way to express 'X is a necessary consequence of Y, direction-neutral on Y's truth' without either overloading Ground or adding a converse edge Y->X (which draw-ground also permits and would look like circular mutual support). Same friction n045 apparently hit and left ungrounded.

Connecting the orphan n046 (Abhyankar-Moh-Suzuki embedding theorem) to the base-point-at-infinity thread (n061/n043) required a node (n066) whose relation to n046 is a shared-proof-technique ANALOGY (both are single-point-at-infinity Newton-Puiseux/approximate-root analyses), not logical support: n046 doesn't supply a premise n066 needs to be true, it supplies a template/precedent for HOW to analyze the single-base-point case. draw-ground from n046 to n066 reads as 'n046 is evidence/reason for n066', which overstates it -- the real relation is closer to 'n046 is a structurally analogous, technique-transferring precedent for n066', a methodological-kinship relation the Ground/Dependent grammar has no separate slot for. I used draw-ground anyway (closest fit) rather than leave n046 orphaned, but flagging that this is a distinct relation from ordinary support.

VERIFY-CONVENTION.md §3 instructs builders to append each check's source_entry to harness/sources.json verbatim, but this round's operational brief says 'do NOT edit sources.json' -- a direct contradiction. I edited it once, caught the conflict, and reverted (git checkout) rather than guess which instruction wins. With ~5 concurrent lane agents each running checks, if everyone follows the written VERIFY-CONVENTION.md literally, sources.json becomes a shared-file write-race; the round brief's ban is probably right (centralized registration avoids the race) but the two docs should be reconciled so future rounds don't have to resolve it ad hoc mid-task.

The 350-char soft-cap on claim text is in real tension with BRIEF-jacobian.md §6's demand for explicit quantifiers/field/degree/dimension on every claim: a fully-quantified statement of a closed-form Jacobian-determinant criterion (matrix shape, nilpotency condition, the formula itself, the Keller-iff condition, and the citation tying it to the n=2 base case) routinely runs 400-600 chars. All three claim nodes I created this round (n068 fits, n073/n079 don't) tripped the warning; I kept full precision over trimming to fit, since a vaguer claim would be the actual failure mode the brief warns against.

The most honest next step after finding a criterion vacuous on two families (n074, n077) is a genuinely open, conjectural refinement (track the base-locus resolution under blow-up, not raw leading-form non-degeneracy) -- but the grammar has no way to mark a claim as 'open research direction, not asserted as fact' short of hedging language inside the claim text itself, which then reads oddly next to sharply-quantified checkable claims. A distinct node type or scope-tag for 'open direction' (vs. 'claim I'm asserting') would let n078 be stated as a question rather than a hedged assertion.

Every claim this round that states an explicit, checkable criterion (field, degree, dimension, quantifiers) tripped the 350-char soft-cap warning (n071: 439, n074: 556, n077: 423 chars) -- precision and brevity pull opposite directions here, and BRIEF-jacobian.md explicitly asks for explicit quantifiers/degree/dimension. Not blocking (soft cap), but the cap seems calibrated for looser prose claims, not for reformulation nodes carrying a full mathematical statement.

Precision repair has no cheap primitive: correcting an imprecise-but-not-wholly-false claim (n024/n025's kernel description, missing the other n-1 variables) forces a full supersede+new-node+manual-reground cycle even though most of the original phrasing's intent survives. propose-phrasing is honest only when the proposition is literally the same; here the corrected quantifiers change the proposition's truth value, so a new node was the honest choice, but the grammar has no lighter 'amend-and-keep-history' move between propose-phrasing and full supersede.

New rule 'if a check refutes an existing claim node, ground it there and rate the refuted claim low' has no path when the refuted node is the rater's own: self-rating is rejected by the engine. supersede --reason plus a comment is the only available mechanism in that case; the rule as worded assumes the refuted node belongs to someone else.

The 350-char soft cap on claim text is in real tension with brief section 6's 'make quantifiers, field, degree, dimension explicit' -- every precise ring-theoretic statement in this round (ker(d/dx_i) = k[...][x_i^p], Euler's-identity conditionals) tripped the cap by 1.5-2x. Precision and brevity pull opposite ways for this problem domain; a claim can be quantifier-exact or under 350 chars but rarely both.

Grounding n091 (general biconditional: for the whole cubic-homogeneous/JH-nilpotent reduced class, termination iff Picard-truncation plateau) into n092 (the same 'only if' direction restated as a conditional specifically about Keller maps, i.e. instances where the antecedent is known to be satisfiable) used draw-ground, but the actual relation is a scope-specialization/instantiation of the same proposition, not independent evidential support -- n092 doesn't add a new reason to believe the plateau characterization, it reapplies n091 to a named subclass so it reads as a standalone conditional per the round's scope-tagging convention. The grammar has no 'specializes/instantiates' relation distinct from Ground, so this had to be encoded as support, which slightly overstates it as independent corroboration rather than a restatement at narrower scope.

Grounding n093/n094/n095/n044/n020 into n098 ('properness is the sole unverifiable hypothesis in the chain') uses the same plain Ground edge as ordinary inferential support, but the actual relationship is different in kind: each of n093/n094/n095 is cited as ONE INSTANCE substantiating a universally-quantified structural claim ('every step in this chain is an unconditional theorem except properness') rather than as a premise from which n098 is inferred. An itemized enumeration backing a for-all-steps claim and a normal lemma-to-conclusion step render identically as Ground edges, even though the former is closer to exhaustion-style support (like the exhaustion certificate format) than to deductive inference. The grammar has no way to mark 'this ground is one case of an enumerated whole' versus 'this ground is a premise the conclusion follows from.'

n098 ('properness is the sole unverifiable hypothesis in the n016/n017-to-n044 route') is a claim ABOUT the shape and diagnostic value of an argument chain already in the graph (a proof-strategy assessment: 'this route contributes zero independent leverage'), not a first-order mathematical claim about Keller maps. It is authored and rated as an ordinary  node on the same Agreement dial as e.g. n016 or n044, but its truth-conditions are one level up (they concern which hypothesis in a specific decomposition is load-bearing), and its evidence is 'the structure of nodes n093/n094/n095/n017/n044/n020 as drawn' rather than mathematics external to the graph. The grammar has no node kind or relation for a self-referential meta-claim about a proof's architecture, distinct from a claim about the domain the proof is in; both currently look identical.

It is authored and rated as an ordinary claim node on the same Agreement dial as e.g. n016 or n044, but its truth-conditions are one level up: they concern which hypothesis in a specific decomposition is load-bearing, and its evidence is the structure of nodes n093/n094/n095/n017/n044/n020 as drawn, rather than mathematics external to the graph. The grammar has no node kind or relation for a self-referential meta-claim about a proof's architecture, distinct from a claim about the domain the proof is in; both currently look identical.

draw-ground reads identically whether a Ground is a deductive step or a finite set of confirming instances feeding an inductive generalization. n100 states a universal law ('for all p prime, d>=2, n>=1, F=X-H is Keller iff p|d') grounded by n099 (10 explicit (p,d) exhaustion cases) and n085 (one prior instance) -- but neither edge can express 'this is exhaustive support for a stated-universal claim over an unbounded family, verified only on finitely many small cases' versus a genuine proof. A reader can't tell from Ground structure alone whether a general-law node is proven or merely illustrated by its grounds; this recurs across the whole computational-exploration lane (brief lane 4) and is worth a dedicated edge attribute or a convention (e.g. a title prefix) rather than leaving it implicit in claim text.

This round's mandate referred to n011 as 'the Dixmier/mod-p transfer thread opener, currently isolated,' but n011's actual content (naive one-step inverse guess fails at nilpotency index >=3) has nothing to do with Dixmier/Weyl-algebra mod-p transfer -- n010 (titled 'Lane opener: formal inverse as tree expansion') is the much closer match, and no Dixmier-themed node existed in the graph at all before this round. The grammar has no way to mark 'this node is the canonical opener/anchor of thread X' independent of prose title text, so a lane's own sense of where its threads live drifts across rounds/restarts and can only be reconciled by re-reading full node text rather than querying structure -- I grounded the new node (n104) into both n010 and n011 to hedge, but that's a workaround, not a fix.

Two recurring gaps hit again this round, both instances of a class f10 already named: (1) No 'supersededBy' edge. supersede --target n065 --reason '...' records the demotion and I wrote the replacement's id (n108) into the free-text reason and a comment, but there is no structural edge type for 'this ghost's honest content now lives at node Y' -- a reader who lists ghosts sees n065 demoted with a reason, but the graph has no traversable link from n065 to n108 the way draw-ground would give one; the connection exists only in prose. (2) 'Comparandum/background', not support. I drew n061->n106, n063->n106, and n063->n107 with draw-ground, but the true relation is not 'n061/n063 are reasons to believe n106/n107' -- it's 'n061/n063 establish the baseline pattern against which n106/n107's finding is legible and gets its significance as a finding at all.' Grounding reads as premise-for-conclusion; what I actually mean is closer to 'necessary interpretive context.' Same shape as f10's methodological-kinship gap, but here the two nodes being related are both mine and both genuinely load-bearing claims (not an orphan-adoption case), so this isn't a one-off -- it looks like a general missing relation between a claim and the baseline/precedent that gives it meaning.

n079 (Keller<=>triangular pattern observed in a 6-matrix sample, explicitly scoped to n=3) and n111 (the same pattern breaks at n=4: a non-triangular yet Keller instance exists) are not really rivals in the antithesis sense -- n079 stays TRUE as stated, correctly bounded to n=3; n111 doesn't contradict it, it shows the pattern doesn't generalize past its stated bound. The actual relation is 'this narrow verified sample does not extend to the next dimension' -- a scope-qualification/generalization-boundary relation, distinct from both support and rivalry-over-the-same-proposition. I used add-antithesis (set s2) as the nearest fit since both are positive claims addressing the same underlying question ('does nilpotency index 3 alone force triangularizability?'), but the fit is imperfect: unlike a normal antithesis pair, one member here doesn't need to be wrong for the other to be right.

Drew e142 (n113 -> n073, ground) to connect the new explicit-inverse-formula claim to the det-formula claim about the same rank-1 nilpotent family, but on reflection this isn't really argumentative support (n113 doesn't make n073 more likely true) -- it's a companion/adjacent structural fact about the same object (one gives det JF, the other gives F^{-1}), useful to a reader navigating the family but not evidence FOR the Keller-criterion claim. The grammar has Grounds (support) and Antitheses (rivalry) but no 'about the same construction, here's a related fact' relation; I used Ground as the nearest fit since there is no delete and leaving it structurally unattached (orphaned) seemed worse, but the edge slightly overclaims what the relation is.

The loop's 'rival, don't negate' step had no natural target this round: n119 (properness = growth = bounded fibers), n122 (asymptotic-variety mechanism), and n124 (escape direction hits a base point) are settled classical facts / direct logical corollaries of already-established nodes (n020/n044, n043/n071), not contested positions with a genuine competing positive claim. Forcing an antithesis onto uncontested math would misrepresent it as split when it isn't -- but the grammar has no way to record 'this node has no rival because the mathematics settles it,' which is different from silently skipping the step. A lightweight 'uncontested' marker (distinct from simply omitting antithesis membership) would let a reader distinguish 'nobody got around to rivaling this' from 'this genuinely admits no rival.'

draw-ground into an evidence node (e.g. n121 -> n097, both citing exact machine-checked transcripts about the SAME underlying map, F(x,y)=(e^x cos y, e^x sin y)) triggers a discouragement warning ('Evidence is anchored by its source, not grounded by in-graph reasoning'), but there is no other grammar element for 'these two independently-sourced evidence nodes concern the same mathematical object, and the second extends the first with a further exact computation.' It isn't a support relation (n121's curve computation doesn't make n097's fiber-cardinality claim more true) and it isn't rivalry. I left the edge in place since the warning is advisory, but the graph now has no clean way to mark 'same-object elaboration' between two evidence nodes short of either the discouraged ground or an unlinked pair that a reader has to notice share a map by re-reading both texts.

Grounding n019/n044 (abstract topological equivalence: 'proper Keller map = polynomial automorphism') into n131/n133 (the geometric/computational translations of the same dichotomy into blow-up and fiber-cardinality vocabulary) is not evidential support in the ordinary sense -- n019 doesn't supply a premise that n131 infers from, and n131 isn't a further logical step past n019. The real relation is OPERATIONALIZATION: n131/n133 restate n019/n044's content in a different, independently-checkable representation (resolved-exceptional-locus geometry; fiber cardinality) so that a specific instance can be machine-verified, without adding or removing logical content. draw-ground is the closest verb but reads as 'n019 is a reason to believe n131', which overstates a translation as an inference. This is a distinct shape from f7's exemplar/universal mismatch and from f10/f24's methodological-kinship and comparandum-context gaps -- those are about missing STRENGTH-of-support information; this is about the edge asserting a support relation where the real relation is a change of representation with no strength question to ask at all.

Scope-refinement vs. evidential support are conflated by Ground/Dependent. n090 states the Picard-plateau law narrowly (shift-nilpotent chains, index=p=n); n138 generalizes it to instances where index<n (n110's non-triangular conjugate, n073/n113's rank-1 case), and n139 states the fully general conditional. I drew n090 -> n138 as a Ground edge, but the real relation is 'n138 subsumes/generalizes n090's scope, using new evidence n090 didn't have' -- not 'n090 is a reason to believe n138' (n090's specific instances are strictly implied by n138, not separately evidential for it). The grammar has no verb for 'this claim is a scope-broadening of that one, and the earlier claim survives as a corollary' -- Ground reads identically whether the edge means 'reason for' or 'special case subsumed by'. Same issue applies to n091/n092's plateau-vs-non-termination split, which the round brief itself flags as adjacent to the gated scope-tagging feature (SPEC S6.3).

Drew, then had to supersede (e190, e191), two Ground edges from n141 (a converse mod-p instance) into n104 and n010 -- the brief's mandate literally said 'grounding into the Dixmier-connection claims', but on reflection neither edge was actually a REASON-FOR relationship: n141 is a complementary/contrasting data point in the same investigation as n104, not evidence that n104 or n010 are true (or false). The grammar has no relation for 'these two claims are about the same sub-question and worth reading together' short of Ground (asserts support) or Antithesis (asserts rivalry) -- this is neither. I ended up leaving n141 topically adjacent only via its own legitimate grounds (n137, n035), but a reader has no structural signal that n141 and n104 are part of the same thread except node text cross-references by id. A 'see-also / same-topic' edge distinct from Ground/Antithesis would have fit this case cleanly.

Repeatedly hit the 350-char soft cap on claims that genuinely bundle two tightly-coupled sub-results from one investigation (n134: 'p|deg H is structurally sufficient for power-of-linear-form H, AND is not exhaustive/not general for other H'; n144: 'collision does not persist across two distinct tested instances, AND the load-bearing factor looks like integer-invertibility rather than diagonality'). Splitting each into two atomic nodes would sever findings that only mean something as a contrast pair -- exactly the case the Conjunction-group mechanism handles for GROUNDS jointly supporting one dependent, but there is no analogous device for a single claim's own text being a natural two-part contrast. I used propose-phrasing to add a tighter alternative wording rather than truly decomposing, which reduces the char count but doesn't resolve the underlying tension -- the two halves are still one proposition ('X holds in case A but not case B') that resists being cut without loss.

Two related gaps surfaced doing computational-exploration work this round. (1) The certificate-format contract's 'exhaustion: finite search with stated bounds' collapses two epistemically different situations into the same node text: a DELIBERATELY chosen sample size (e.g. chk-l4-13's fixed 100) vs. a sweep FORCED to truncate mid-run by an unplanned wall-clock budget (chk-l4-15 attempted 60, bailed at 51 when a single slow determinant blew the internal time budget). Both render as 'N samples, stated bound' prose: nothing in the grammar marks that the second case's remaining untested samples were skipped by resource exhaustion rather than by design, which matters for how much a reader should trust that the untested tail behaves like the tested head. (2) Frequency/statistical claims over a randomized sample (n118, n146: '27% of Keller instances in this seeded sweep are non-triangular') have a different epistemic character than a universal or exhaustive claim, but claim nodes have no way to mark 'this is a sampled-frequency estimate, re-running with a different seed could shift the number' as distinct from a proof-like or fully-exhaustive claim -- a reader has to infer that from prose alone.

n152 needed to state a three-leg iff-chain (proper <-> no escape curve <-> no base-locus escape) where each leg's TWO directions have different proof status: leg (i) both directions unconditional theorem, leg (ii) both directions the JC-equivalent theorem, leg (iii) one direction proven (n124) and its converse open (equivalent to JC). The grammar has no structural way to mark a Ground edge as 'this direction only' of a biconditional, or to distinguish a proven implication from its open converse except by writing it out in prose inside one claim's text -- draw-ground is a single directed arrow with no modal/status tag (proven/open/instance-only), so the whole asymmetric status profile had to be encoded as unstructured text in n152 rather than as separate, individually-gradable edges. A 'converse-of' or 'status:{theorem|open|instance-verified}' tag on Ground edges would let a reader/rater engage with just the open leg instead of the whole compound claim.

chk-l3-11/chk-l3-12 use symbolic (not numeric) leading coefficients across finitely many degree-profile cases (k0=1..6), which actually PROVES the no-escape-curve result for every bounded-Laurent-degree curve up to D=6 -- not merely a sampled instance check -- yet BRIEF-jacobian.md's certificate taxonomy (identity / invertibility / non-injectivity / exhaustion) has no name for this middle case: stronger than a numeric exhaustion (it is uniform over all coefficient values), weaker than a fully general theorem (it is finite in the degree parameter D). I wrote the claim text (n148/n155) as a 'scoped absence-claim, D<=6' per the round mandate's suggested framing, but that undersells what was actually verified (a closed argument, degree-parametrized) versus what a pure random-sample exhaustion would show; a 'parametrized/uniform exhaustion' certificate label would let readers calibrate confidence correctly instead of reading it as coarser than it is.

n157 has to bundle two epistemically different statuses into one claim's text: a theorem-like half (denominator-clearing/chain-rule forces well-definedness, Keller-ness, and inverse-survival for p outside D -- provable from commutative algebra alone) and a verified-instance-only half (that D is the EXACT exceptional set, not merely a safe upper bound -- confirmed on 4 instances, n104 plus three new rational-coefficient checks this round, but not proved in general). The grammar has no way to mark part of a claim's text as 'established by argument' and another part as 'established by finite verification, extrapolated' -- both live as prose inside one node, so a reader has to trust the author's own labeling ('THEOREM-LIKE' / 'VERIFIED-INSTANCE-ONLY' as literal words in the claim) rather than the graph structure surfacing the distinction. Splitting into two separate nodes felt worse: the two halves are not independent claims, they are two epistemic registers of the SAME conditional, and splitting would suggest they're separately contestable when really one is settled math and the other is an open empirical question about the same statement.

The VERIFY-CONVENTION's TRUE/FALSE/INCONCLUSIVE verdict space doesn't have a native slot for 'mod-p reduction of this map is UNDEFINED at p' (chk-l5-15/16/17's p=2, p=3, p in {2,3} cases). That's not the same kind of fact as 'reduces fine but fails to be Keller' (n099/n100's char-p mechanism) -- it's a prior-stage fact about whether the reduction map itself exists, before Keller-ness is even askable. I folded it into the same TRUE verdict by treating 'exceptional set = predicted denominator-prime set' as the checked claim (so a p where reduction is undefined counts as a correctly-predicted member of the bad set, same bucket as a p where reduction succeeds but collides) -- but structurally these are different failure kinds (non-existence of the reduced object vs. existence-but-non-injectivity), and a reader scanning verdicts alone can't tell which one a given exceptional prime represents without reading the transcript.

A VERDICT:FALSE transcript is binary, but the checks in this session (chk-l1-14) showed two structurally different ways a compound CLAIM can fail: (a) the premise fails (A nilpotent but F not even Keller -- refutes an over-generalization about the construction, n034) vs (b) the premise holds but the law's conclusion fails (F is Keller but Picard plateau deviates from index/degree -- would refute n138/n140 itself). The runner's TRUE/FALSE marker and the resulting [REFUTED] source_entry don't distinguish these; routing the evidence to the right dependent (the premise-claim it actually defeats, not the law under test) required re-deriving that distinction by hand from the transcript body. A structured verdict (e.g. PREMISE_FAILED vs CONCLUSION_FAILED, or just requiring compound claims to be split into separately-verified conjuncts) would make the routing mechanical instead of an inference the builder has to redo every time.

Correcting a PAST ROUND's own claim (n063/n064, authored by this same agent id in an earlier round) exposed a gap: supersede + comment + antithesis works structurally, but the self-rate restriction (cannot rate your own authored node) means the corrected picture never gets the low Agreement score that would sink n063/n064 toward a ghost through the normal rating mechanism -- supersede's --reason field is the only place the refutation is recorded as anything other than a sibling antithesis node. For a persistent multi-round agent identity, 'my own past claim was wrong' seems to need a stronger structural move than what's available to other-agent refutations, where a rating from the discovering agent naturally does the sinking.

A bounded negative-existence search (chk-l2-13: tried 3 representative rank-2 forms up to degree 8, none completed to a Keller pair, vs. a working rank-1 control) is genuinely different in evidential weight from BRIEF's 'exhaustion' certificate format (a finite, EXHAUSTIVE search over a STATED, complete family) -- it's suggestive, not exhaustive, since the space of possible completions (any h, any degree) is unbounded. The grammar has no way to mark a claim's evidential status as 'bounded-but-not-exhaustive negative search' distinctly from a genuine exhaustion certificate or a proof; I wrote n171's claim text to hedge this explicitly ('suggesting', 'may'), but that's prose discipline, not a structural distinction the graph records anywhere (e.g. on the evidence node's kind/quality).

Epistemic-kind blur in 'evidence supports claim' grounds: n177's evidence (chk-l4-17) shows a rank-2 determinant identity's Q2==0-AND-Q4==0 condition matches Keller on 60/60 sample instances -- but that match is GUARANTEED by algebra once the identity det(JF)=1+Q2+Q4 is confirmed on even one instance (it's a tautological reformulation of det(JF)=const, not an independent hypothesis that could have failed on the other 59). Contrast n178's evidence (chk-l4-18): its zero-row/zero-column/etc invariants are genuinely independent structural guesses that COULD have separated the sample and didn't. Both render as identical 'evidence -> claim, N/N match' grounds, but one is a correctness check on an algebraic derivation and the other is real inductive/exhaustive support for a substantive empirical claim. The grammar has no way to mark 'this match was guaranteed by construction' vs 'this match was a real test the hypothesis could have failed' -- a reader comparing n177 and n178's grounds side by side would reasonably (and wrongly) read them as epistemically equivalent.

n180 combines two propositions that only mean much together: 'the rank-2 chain-functional criterion DOES separate Keller' (positive, from n177+n179) and 'simple structural invariants do NOT separate it' (contrastive, from n178). Splitting into two claim nodes would lose exactly the point -- the criterion's interest lies in it being the thing that succeeds where the obvious guesses fail, a genuinely comparative proposition, not two independent facts that happen to share a topic. Cramming them into one node (579 chars, over the soft cap) was the least-bad option, but it isn't really 'one proposition' in the Rule-1 sense either. This is the same shape as the AGENT-GUIDE's conjunction-group mechanism (grounds that only mean much jointly) but inverted: here it's the CLAIM, not the grounds, that is irreducibly comparative against a rejected alternative -- there's no first-class way to say 'X, as opposed to the more obvious Y which fails' without folding Y's failure into X's own text.
