typings.author: builder-l1
typings.label: independent replication: the same closed-form inverse is re-derived via a different method (Picard-iteration convergence) rather than the original direct construction/composition check

meta.question: Is the Jacobian Conjecture true? (Is every polynomial map C^n -> C^n with constant nonzero Jacobian determinant invertible with polynomial inverse?)

phrasings.text: For every n >= 1 and every polynomial map F: C^n -> C^n with det(JF) a nonzero constant (i.e. F is a Keller map), F is a polynomial automorphism of C^n (bijective with polynomial inverse).

titles.text: JC holds: every Keller map is a polynomial automorphism

phrasings.text: There exists some n >= 2 and some polynomial Keller map F: C^n -> C^n (det(JF) a nonzero constant) such that F is not injective on C^n, hence has no polynomial inverse.

titles.text: JC fails: a non-injective Keller map exists

phrasings.text: For every n >= 1, every Keller map F: C^n -> C^n with deg F <= 2 is a polynomial automorphism of C^n (Wang, 1980).

titles.text: Degree <=2 case (Wang 1980)

phrasings.text: For every n, it suffices to prove the Jacobian Conjecture for maps of the form F = X + H: C^n -> C^n where H is cubic homogeneous and JH is nilpotent -- i.e. every instance of the Jacobian Conjecture reduces, after increasing dimension, to this cubic-homogeneous case (Bass-Connell-Wright; Yagzhev, ~1980-82).

titles.text: Cubic-homogeneous reduction (BCW/Yagzhev)

phrasings.text: It suffices to further restrict the cubic-homogeneous reduction to maps of the Druzkowski cubic-linear form F = X + (AX)^{*3} for some n x n nilpotent matrix A, i.e. each component is F_i = x_i + (row_i(A).x)^3 (Druzkowski, 1983).

titles.text: Cubic-linear reduction (Druzkowski 1983)

phrasings.text: For n=2, every Keller map F: C^2 -> C^2 with deg F <= 100 is a polynomial automorphism of C^2 (Moh, 1983).

titles.text: n=2, degree <=100 case (Moh 1983)

phrasings.text: If a Keller map F: C^n -> C^n is invertible with polynomial inverse, then deg(F^{-1}) <= (deg F)^{n-1}; consequently, for any explicit candidate Keller map, 'has a polynomial inverse' is a finitely-checkable property bounded by this degree.

titles.text: Inverse-degree bound makes invertibility finitely checkable

phrasings.text: Over an algebraically closed field of characteristic p>0, the naive analog of the Keller condition does not force injectivity: the polynomial f(x) = x - x^p has formal derivative f'(x) = 1 identically (a nonzero constant) yet f is not injective (f(a)=0 for every a in F_p), so any argument for the Jacobian Conjecture that would apply verbatim in positive characteristic is thereby shown incorrect.

titles.text: Boundary: naive char-p analog is false

phrasings.text: Weakening the Keller hypothesis 'det JF is a nonzero constant' to merely 'det JF is everywhere nonzero' (rather than constant) is false already over R^2: there is an explicit real polynomial map F: R^2 -> R^2 of modest (double-digit) degree with det(JF) nonvanishing everywhere that is not injective (Pinchuk, 1994), so constancy of det JF -- not merely its non-vanishing -- is an essential hypothesis of the conjecture.

titles.text: Boundary: real non-vanishing (vs constant) Jacobian is false (Pinchuk 1994)

phrasings.text: For F = X + H with H cubic homogeneous and JH nilpotent, the formal power-series inverse of F (which always exists by the inverse function theorem) is given by an explicit combinatorial expansion indexed by rooted trees built from H and its derivatives; the Jacobian Conjecture restricted to this reduced class is equivalent to the claim that this tree-indexed expansion has only finitely many nonzero terms (terminates), rather than existing merely as a genuine infinite formal power series.

titles.text: Lane opener: formal inverse as tree expansion; JC = termination

phrasings.text: A natural shortcut is to guess that the formal inverse of F = X + H (H cubic homogeneous, JH nilpotent) is well-approximated by the single one-step candidate G(Y) = Y - H(Y); this already fails to be a two-sided inverse once the nilpotency index of JH is >= 3, so the higher tree-terms of the formal inverse are load-bearing content, not optional low-order corrections.

titles.text: Naive one-step inverse guess fails at nilpotency index >=3

phrasings.text: Verified: F=(x1, x2+x1^2) over C^2 is a Keller map (det JF=1 identically) with explicit degree-2 polynomial inverse G=(y1, y2-y1^2), checked by symbolic composition F(G(y))=y.

source.title: F=(x1, x2+x1^2) over C^2 is a Keller map (det JF=1) with explicit degree-2 polynomial inverse G=(y1, y2-y1^2) verified by composition F(G(y))=y.

titles.text: chk-l1-01: quadratic Keller map, verified inverse

phrasings.text: Verified: the Druzkowski cubic-linear map F=(x1+(x1+x2)^3, x2-(x1+x2)^3) over C^2 (A=[[1,1],[-1,-1]], nilpotent, A^2=0) is a Keller map (det JF=1 identically) with explicit degree-3 polynomial inverse G=(y1-(y1+y2)^3, y2+(y1+y2)^3), checked by symbolic composition, saturating the (deg F)^{n-1} bound.

source.title: The Druzkowski cubic-linear map F=X+(AX)^{*3} over C^2 with A=[[1,1],[-1,-1]] (nilpotent, A^2=0), i.e. F=(x1+(x1+x2)^3, x2-(x1+x2)^3), is a Keller map (det JF=1) with explicit degree-3 polynomial inverse G=(y1-(y1+y2)^3, y2+(y1+y2)^3) verified by composition, saturating the deg(F)^(n-1) bound.

titles.text: chk-l1-02: cubic-linear Keller map, verified inverse via invariant

phrasings.text: Verified: over F_5, f(x)=x-x^5 has formal derivative f'(x)=1 identically as a polynomial mod 5, yet f(0)=f(1)=0 in F_5, an explicit non-injectivity collision under a constant nonzero derivative.

source.title: Over F_5 (characteristic 5), f(x) = x - x^5 has formal derivative f'(x) = 1 identically (a nonzero constant, the n=1 Keller condition) but f is not injective on F_5: e.g. f(0) = f(1) = 0.

titles.text: chk-l1-03: char-5 collision under constant derivative

phrasings.text: Verified FALSE: for the Keller map F=(x1+x2^3, x2+x3^3, x3) over C^3 (JH nilpotent of index 3), the naive one-step candidate G(y)=(y1-y2^3, y2-y3^3, y3) does NOT compose to the identity -- F(G(y)) != y symbolically, refuting the one-step-guess as a general inversion method.

source.title: [REFUTED] For the Druzkowski/Jordan-block cubic-linear Keller map F=(x1+x2^3, x2+x3^3, x3) over C^3 (JH nilpotent of index 3), the naive one-step candidate G(y)=(y1-y2^3, y2-y3^3, y3) is a two-sided polynomial inverse of F.

titles.text: chk-l1-04: naive one-step inverse guess refuted

phrasings.text: Since a Keller map F: C^n -> C^n has det(JF) a nonzero constant, F is a local biholomorphism (local complex-analytic isomorphism) at every point of C^n by the holomorphic inverse function theorem; equivalently, viewed as a real-analytic map on R^{2n}, F is a local diffeomorphism everywhere.

titles.text: Keller maps are everywhere local biholomorphisms

phrasings.text: A local diffeomorphism F: M -> N between connected manifolds of equal dimension (N connected, locally compact, Hausdorff) is a covering map onto N if and only if F is proper (preimages of compact sets are compact); consequently if N is additionally simply connected and M is connected, a proper local diffeomorphism F: M -> N is automatically a global diffeomorphism (bijective with smooth/holomorphic inverse), since the only connected covering space of a simply connected base is the trivial one.

titles.text: Properness + local diffeomorphism + simply-connected target implies global diffeomorphism

phrasings.text: C^n is simply connected, so applying the covering-space criterion to a Keller map F: C^n -> C^n: if F is proper (equivalently, |F(x)| -> infinity as |x| -> infinity), then F is a global biholomorphic automorphism of C^n, which is exactly the Jacobian Conjecture's conclusion for that F.

titles.text: Properness of a Keller map implies its bijectivity via the covering-space criterion

phrasings.text: Conversely, if a Keller map F: C^n -> C^n is injective, then (by Ax-Grothendieck, an injective polynomial self-map of C^n is automatically bijective with polynomial inverse) F is a polynomial automorphism, and a polynomial automorphism is a homeomorphism of C^n hence proper; so properness is not merely a sufficient route to the Jacobian Conjecture's conclusion for a given Keller map but is equivalent to it.

titles.text: Bijectivity of a Keller map implies properness (converse direction)

phrasings.text: The Jacobian Conjecture is equivalent to the purely analytic/growth statement: every Keller map F: C^n -> C^n is proper, i.e. |F(x)| -> infinity as |x| -> infinity; JC for a given F holds iff F satisfies this growth condition.

titles.text: JC is equivalent to properness (growth) of Keller maps

phrasings.text: Over C, a polynomial p in C[x_1,...,x_n] that is nonvanishing at every point of C^n is automatically a nonzero constant (Hilbert's Nullstellensatz: the zero set is empty iff p generates the unit ideal iff p is a unit, i.e. a nonzero constant, since C is algebraically closed); consequently for a polynomial map F: C^n -> C^n, 'det JF nonvanishing everywhere on C^n' and 'det JF a nonzero constant' are the SAME hypothesis -- there is no strictly-weaker nonvanishing-only version of the Keller condition to state over C.

titles.text: Over C, nonvanishing det JF already forces det JF constant (Nullstellensatz)

phrasings.text: Verified: for F(x,y)=(x, y*(x^2+1)), det JF = x^2+1 has no real root (F nonvanishing-Jacobian, non-constant, on R^2) but has a root at x=i over C, concretely instantiating why nonvanishing and constant coincide for det JF over C but diverge over R.

source.title: For F(x,y)=(x, y*(x^2+1)), det JF = x^2+1 has no root in R (F is nonvanishing-Jacobian on R^2, non-constant) but has a root at x=i in C, so the same non-constant Jacobian-determinant polynomial cannot be nonvanishing on all of C^2 -- concretely instantiating why 'nonvanishing' and 'constant' Jacobian-determinant hypotheses coincide over C but diverge over R.

titles.text: chk-l3-01: real-nonvanishing-but-non-constant det has a complex root

phrasings.text: Because nonvanishing and constant coincide for det JF over C but not over R, the Pinchuk real counterexample (nonvanishing but non-constant real Jacobian determinant, not injective) has no possible complex analog: its Jacobian determinant, viewed as a polynomial over C, necessarily acquires complex zeros, so it can never be extended to a genuine Keller map over C^n -- the real boundary case is a phenomenon unavailable to any would-be complex counterexample by this algebraic obstruction alone.

titles.text: The Pinchuk-type real counterexample has no possible complex analog

comments.text: Superseded: refuted by chk-l5-05 (n075) -- the char-p kernel is not just the subring generated by x_i^p, it also contains everything not involving x_i. See n070 for the corrected statement.

demoted.reason: Imprecise: 'ker(d/dx_i) = subring generated by x_i^p' omits the other n-1 variables and is refuted by chk-l5-05 (n075), which exhibits kernel elements x_2^3, x_1^5*x_2^2 outside that subring. Corrected by n070.

phrasings.text: Over a field of characteristic p>0, the kernel of the partial derivative operator d/dx_i on k[x_1,...,x_n] is not just constants: it equals the subring generated by x_i^p (since d(x_i^p)/dx_i = p*x_i^{p-1} = 0). A Jacobian in characteristic p therefore cannot detect perturbing any component of F by a polynomial built from p-th powers of the variables, so 'det JF is a nonzero constant' is comparatively easy to satisfy there.

titles.text: Char-p mechanism: derivative kernel is not just constants

comments.text: Superseded: false as stated for n>=2 (any polynomial not involving x_i is a non-constant kernel element, e.g. x_2 in k[x_1,x_2]). Only true restricted to the univariate slice k[x_i] -- verified by chk-l5-04 (n072). See n067 for the corrected statement.

demoted.reason: Imprecise/false for n>=2 as literally stated: 'ker(d/dx_i) = constants k for every i' ignores that any polynomial not involving x_i (e.g. x_j, j!=i) lies in the kernel while non-constant. True only restricted to the univariate slice k[x_i] (chk-l5-04, n072). Corrected by n067.

phrasings.text: Over a field of characteristic 0 (in particular C), the kernel of d/dx_i on k[x_1,...,x_n] is exactly the constants k, for every i: no nonconstant polynomial has an identically vanishing partial derivative. So the Jacobian-invisible perturbation (adding multiples of x_i^p) that defeats the naive char-p analog has no counterpart over C -- that specific failure mechanism is structurally unavailable.

titles.text: Char-0 gap: no derivative-blind perturbation exists

phrasings.text: The mechanism behind Pinchuk's real counterexample is that 'det JF nonvanishing everywhere' gives only that F is a local diffeomorphism at every point; Hadamard's global inverse function theorem needs properness of F in addition to local invertibility to conclude F is a global diffeomorphism, and a nonvanishing-but-non-constant Jacobian does not by itself supply properness.

titles.text: Real mechanism: nonvanishing Jacobian omits properness

phrasings.text: Verified: over F_5, F(x,y) = (x - x^5, y) has Jacobian determinant identically 1 mod 5 (Keller condition holds at n=2) yet F(0,0) = F(1,0) = (0,0) -- a genuinely 2-variable char-p Keller-map collision, extending the existing n=1 example to the dimension where JC is actually non-vacuous.

source.title: Over F_5 (char p=5), the genuinely 2-variable map F(x,y) = (x - x^5, y) has Jacobian determinant identically equal to the constant 1 (mod 5) as a polynomial identity in x,y -- i.e. F satisfies the Keller condition -- yet F(0,0) = F(1,0), an explicit non-injectivity collision, at n=2 (the smallest dimension where the true, char-0 Jacobian Conjecture is non-vacuous).

titles.text: chk-l5-01: 2-variable char-5 Keller-map collision

phrasings.text: Verified: over Q, the identical formula F(x,y) = (x - x^5, y) has Jacobian determinant 1 - 5x^4, which is not constant -- so this construction fails the Keller hypothesis itself in characteristic 0; the char-p blind-spot family is screened out before injectivity is ever tested.

source.title: Over Q (characteristic 0), the identical formula F(x,y) = (x - x^5, y) that is a Keller map when reduced mod 5 has Jacobian determinant 1 - 5*x^4, which is NOT a constant polynomial -- so this construction fails the Keller hypothesis itself over a characteristic-0 field; the char-p counterexample family is screened out at the hypothesis-checking stage, before injectivity is ever tested.

titles.text: chk-l5-02: same formula is not even Keller over Q

phrasings.text: Verified: the smooth (non-polynomial) real map F(x,y) = (e^x cos y, e^x sin y) has det JF = e^(2x), nonvanishing everywhere but not constant, and F(x,y) = F(x,y+2*pi) identically -- an explicit non-injectivity collision illustrating that Jacobian nonvanishing alone never forces global injectivity, independent of Pinchuk's specific polynomial construction.

source.title: The smooth (non-polynomial) real map F(x,y) = (e^x cos y, e^x sin y) has Jacobian determinant det JF = e^{2x}, which is nonvanishing at every real point but is NOT a constant polynomial/function of (x,y); F is nonetheless non-injective because F(x, y) = F(x, y + 2*pi) identically -- an explicit collision -- illustrating that 'Jacobian nonvanishing everywhere' (local invertibility everywhere) does not by itself force global injectivity, independent of any specific polynomial counterexample.

titles.text: chk-l5-03: nonvanishing-but-non-constant Jacobian, periodic collision

phrasings.text: Over C, det JF constant unconditionally gives only that F is a local biholomorphism everywhere -- the same purely local strength Pinchuk's example shows insufficient for global injectivity over R. Any JC proof stopping at local invertibility/etaleness inherits that same gap; if JC is true, the closing ingredient must be strictly stronger and available over C but not R -- plausibly F polynomial combined with det JF LITERALLY constant, e.g. via the inverse-degree bound.

titles.text: Local invertibility alone cannot be what closes JC

phrasings.text: The char-p derivative-blind-spot mechanism is a purely algebraic degeneracy of the derivative operator, structurally absent in characteristic 0 -- but its absence is necessary, not sufficient, for JC: characteristic 0 rules out THIS specific failure mechanism, not every conceivable one, so 'no char-p-style collision is possible' does not by itself establish injectivity.

titles.text: Absence of the char-p mechanism is necessary, not sufficient

phrasings.text: Ruling out the known char-p and real-nonvanishing failure mechanisms for a Keller map over C narrows where a proof or counterexample of JC must live, but does not by itself resolve JC: each ruled-out mechanism closes one specific gap (derivative-blindness; non-properness from mere non-vanishing), not the general possibility that a global local-biholomorphism over C fails to be injective.

titles.text: Boundary analysis narrows, but does not resolve, JC

phrasings.text: Verified: for F=(x1+x2^3, x2+x3^3, x3) (the n=3, nilpotency-index-3 Keller map whose naive one-step guess n015 refuted), the triangular back-substitution map G=(y1-(y2-y3^3)^3, y2-y3^3, y3) is an exact two-sided polynomial inverse, of total degree 9, exactly saturating the (deg F)^(n-1)=3^2=9 bound.

source.title: For the n=3 Keller map F=(x1+x2^3, x2+x3^3, x3) (det JF=1, JH nilpotent of index 3 -- the map whose naive one-step inverse guess G(y)=y-H(y) was verified FALSE), the triangular back-substitution map G=(y1-(y2-y3^3)^3, y2-y3^3, y3) IS an exact two-sided polynomial inverse (F(G(y))=y and G(F(x))=x identically), its total degree is 9, exactly saturating the inverse-degree bound (deg F)^(n-1) = 3^2 = 9.

titles.text: chk-l3-02: terminating true inverse for the n=3 nilpotency-index-3 map, saturating the degree bound

comments.text: Refuted by chk-l4-01 (n035): A=[[2,-4],[1,-2]] is nilpotent (A^2=0) but det JF is not constant. Nilpotency of A is not sufficient for the Druzkowski template to yield a Keller map -- see n036/n037 (chk-l4-02) for the sharper characterization within an explicit finite family.

phrasings.text: A natural strengthening of the Druzkowski cubic-linear reduction is that for every n and every nilpotent n x n matrix A over C (A^k = 0 for some k), the map F = X + (AX)^{*3}: C^n -> C^n is automatically a Keller map, i.e. det(JF) is a nonzero constant merely because A is nilpotent.

titles.text: Over-generalization: nilpotent A alone suffices for Druzkowski Keller-ness

phrasings.text: Verified FALSE: for the nilpotent 2x2 integer matrix A=[[2,-4],[1,-2]] (A^2=0), the Druzkowski cubic-linear map F=X+(AX)^{*3} over C^2 has det(JF)=18x1^2-72x1x2+72x2^2+1, which is NOT constant -- so F is not a Keller map, refuting the claim that nilpotency of A alone suffices.

source.title: [REFUTED] For A=[[2,-4],[1,-2]] (2x2 integer, nilpotent with A^2=0), the Druzkowski cubic-linear map F=X+(AX)^{*3} over C^2 is a Keller map (det JF is a nonzero constant).

titles.text: chk-l4-01: nilpotent A but non-constant det JF (Druzkowski template)

phrasings.text: Within the explicit finite family of the 32 distinct rank<=1 nilpotent 2x2 integer matrices A = u v^T (u, v ranging over {-2,...,2}^2 \ {0} with v.u=0, deduplicated by A), nilpotency of A (A^2=0) is necessary but not sufficient for the Druzkowski cubic-linear map F = X + (AX)^{*3}: C^2 -> C^2 to be a Keller map: exactly 24 of the 32 matrices give det(JF) a nonzero constant and 8 do not.

titles.text: Exhaustion: 24/32 rank<=1 nilpotent 2x2 A's give a Keller map

phrasings.text: Verified: exhaustive computation over the 32 distinct rank<=1 nilpotent 2x2 integer matrices A=u v^T (u,v in {-2,...,2}^2\{0}, v.u=0, deduplicated) finds exactly 24 give det(JF)=const (Keller) for F=X+(AX)^{*3} over C^2, and 8 give a non-constant det(JF).

source.title: Among the 32 distinct rank<=1 nilpotent 2x2 integer matrices A=u v^T with u,v in {-2,...,2}^2\{0} and v.u=0, exactly 24 yield a Keller map F=X+(AX)^{*3} over C^2 and 8 do not.

titles.text: chk-l4-02: exhaustion count 24 Keller / 8 non-Keller in the 32-matrix family

phrasings.text: For any polynomial map F=(P,Q): C^2 -> C^2 with det(JF) a nonzero constant and deg P + deg Q > 2, let P_p, Q_q be the top-degree homogeneous parts of P, Q (of degrees p,q). Then the binary forms P_p, Q_q have identically vanishing Jacobian: (dP_p/dx)(dQ_q/dy) - (dP_p/dy)(dQ_q/dx) = 0 in C[x,y].

titles.text: Leading forms of a 2D Keller map have vanishing Jacobian

phrasings.text: Restricted to the 24 Keller matrices within the 32-matrix rank<=1 nilpotent family of n036, the naive one-step candidate G(Y) = Y - (AY)^{*3} is, in every one of the 24 cases, the exact two-sided polynomial inverse of F = X + (AX)^{*3}: C^2 -> C^2 -- i.e. at nilpotency index exactly 2, restricted to genuine Keller instances, the one-step guess is not merely an approximation but exact, unlike the index>=3 failures already shown.

titles.text: At nilpotency index 2 (Keller case), the one-step inverse guess is exact

phrasings.text: For nonzero homogeneous binary forms f,g in C[x,y] of degrees p,q, the Jacobian J(f,g) = f_x g_y - f_y g_x is identically zero if and only if there is a homogeneous form h of degree d = gcd(p,q) and constants c1,c2 in C^* with f = c1 h^(p/d), g = c2 h^(q/d) -- f,g are each, up to scalar, an exact power of one common form.

titles.text: Vanishing-Jacobian lemma for binary forms (common-power criterion)

phrasings.text: Verified: for all 24 Keller matrices A in the 32-matrix rank<=1 nilpotent 2x2 family (chk-l4-02/n037), the naive candidate G(Y)=Y-(AY)^{*3} composes with F=X+(AX)^{*3} to the identity exactly, checked symbolically for every one of the 24 cases.

source.title: Among the 24 Keller matrices in the stated 32-matrix rank<=1 nilpotent family (chk-l4-02), the naive one-step candidate G(Y)=Y-(AY)^{*3} is the exact two-sided polynomial inverse of F=X+(AX)^{*3} in every case, verified by symbolic composition.

titles.text: chk-l4-03: naive one-step formula exact on all 24 Keller instances

phrasings.text: Two homogeneous binary forms can share the same set of linear-factor directions (the same root set in P^1) without having vanishing Jacobian, if their multiplicities along those shared directions are not all in one common ratio: e.g. f=(x-y)^2(x+2y), g=(x-y)(x+2y)^2 share both roots yet J(f,g) is not identically zero.

titles.text: Shared root set is not sufficient for vanishing Jacobian

phrasings.text: For any Keller map F=(P,Q): C^2 -> C^2 with deg P=p>=1, deg Q=q>=1, p+q>2, the leading forms P_p,Q_q share a common factor of positive degree d=gcd(p,q); equivalently, the projective extension of F to P^2 has at least one base point on the line at infinity Z=0.

titles.text: 2D Keller maps of degree > 1 always have a base point at infinity

phrasings.text: A Keller map F: C^n -> C^n is a polynomial automorphism of C^n if and only if F is a proper map (preimages of compact sets under F are compact); equivalently, the Jacobian Conjecture is exactly the statement that every Keller map C^n -> C^n is proper.

titles.text: JC is equivalent to: every Keller map is proper

phrasings.text: If F: C^n -> C^n is a Keller map that is not a polynomial automorphism (a hypothetical Jacobian-Conjecture counterexample), then F fails to be proper: there is a sequence x_k in C^n with |x_k| -> infinity while F(x_k) stays in some bounded subset of C^n.

titles.text: A hypothetical JC counterexample would have to fail properness

phrasings.text: Abhyankar-Moh-Suzuki embedding theorem: if (P(t),Q(t)): C -> C^2 is a polynomial embedding of the affine line (C[P,Q] = C[t]), then some polynomial automorphism of C^2 carries the image curve to the standard line {y=0}; in particular deg P divides deg Q or deg Q divides deg P.

titles.text: Abhyankar-Moh-Suzuki: embedded lines in C^2 are standard

phrasings.text: Verified: F = G∘H∘L (a genuinely non-triangular degree-6 Keller map on C^2, L linear, H,G elementary) has det JF = 1 identically, and its top-degree homogeneous parts P_top=(x+y)^2, Q_top=(x+y)^6 have J(P_top,Q_top)=0, checked by symbolic differentiation.

source.title: For F = G o H o L (a genuinely non-triangular, non-axis-aligned degree-6 Keller map on C^2), det JF is a nonzero constant AND the Jacobian of the top-degree homogeneous parts of its two components vanishes identically.

titles.text: chk-l2-01: non-triangular Keller map, leading forms confirmed

phrasings.text: Verified: for h=(x-y)(x+2y), f=h^2 (deg4), g=h^3 (deg6) (an exact common-power pair, gcd(4,6)=2=deg h), J(f,g)=0 identically, checked by symbolic differentiation.

source.title: For homogeneous binary forms f = h^2, g = h^3 built from a common non-linear form h = (x-y)(x+2y) (so f,g are each an exact power of one shared form, matching gcd(deg f,deg g)=deg h), the Jacobian J(f,g) = f_x g_y - f_y g_x is identically zero.

titles.text: chk-l2-02: common-power binary forms have vanishing Jacobian

phrasings.text: Verified: f=(x-y)^2(x+2y), g=(x-y)(x+2y)^2 share the same linear-factor root set but J(f,g)=9(x-y)^2(x+2y)^2, not identically zero, checked by symbolic differentiation and factoring.

source.title: f=(x-y)^2(x+2y) and g=(x-y)(x+2y)^2 share the same SET of linear-factor directions ({x=y},{x=-2y}) with only their relative multiplicities swapped, yet J(f,g) is NOT identically zero -- sharing the same root set is not sufficient for a vanishing Jacobian.

titles.text: chk-l2-03: shared-root-set forms with nonzero Jacobian

phrasings.text: Verified FALSE: P=x^2+y, Q=x^3+y (leading forms x^2,x^3 share the common factor x) has det JF = -3x^2+2x, not a nonzero constant; a sweep of 8 further generic low-order completions all failed too -- vanishing leading-form Jacobian is necessary but not sufficient for Keller.

source.title: [REFUTED] The specific low-order completion P=x^2+y, Q=x^3+y of the shared-leading-factor pair (x^2, x^3) is a Keller map on C^2 (det JF a nonzero constant).

titles.text: chk-l2-04 [REFUTED]: naive completion of shared-leading-factor pair is not Keller

phrasings.text: Vanishing Jacobian of a 2D Keller map's leading forms is necessary but not sufficient for the Keller condition: a generic low-order completion of two leading forms that share a common factor need not produce det JF = a nonzero constant, so the leading-form condition alone never certifies invertibility.

titles.text: Leading-form vanishing Jacobian is necessary, not sufficient

phrasings.text: For any rank-1 nilpotent 2x2 matrix A = u v^T over C with u=(a,b), v=(c,d), b!=0, and a*c+b*d=0 (so A^2=0 identically), the Druzkowski cubic-linear map F=X+(AX)^{*3}: C^2 -> C^2 is a Keller map if and only if a*c*(a-b)*(a+b) = 0; equivalently det(JF) = 1 + 3*a*c^3*(a-b)*(a+b)*(b*x1-a*x2)^2/b^2 identically.

titles.text: Closed-form: when a rank-1 nilpotent Druzkowski map (n=2) is Keller

phrasings.text: Verified identity: for A=u v^T (u=(a,b), v=(c,-a*c/b), b!=0, so A^2=0 identically), det(JF)-1 = 3*a*c^3*(a-b)*(a+b)*(b*x1-a*x2)^2/b^2 exactly as a rational-function identity, for the Druzkowski map F=X+(AX)^{*3} over C^2 -- confirmed by exact symbolic simplification (difference reduces to 0).

source.title: For A=u v^T (u=(a,b),v=(c,-a*c/b), b!=0, A^2=0) over C(a,b,c), det(JF)-1 = 3*a*c^3*(a-b)*(a+b)*(b*x1-a*x2)^2/b^2 identically as a rational-function identity in a,b,c,x1,x2, for F=X+(AX)^{*3}: C^2 -> C^2. Consequently F is Keller iff a*c*(a-b)*(a+b)=0.

titles.text: chk-l4-05: exact closed form for det JF of a rank-1 Druzkowski map

phrasings.text: Verified: for F = X + (AX)^{*3}: C^2 -> C^2, in a finite sweep of four non-nilpotent 2x2 matrices A (identity, diag(2,0), upper-unipotent, rank-1-trace-2), det JF is NOT a nonzero constant in every case (F fails Keller), while two nilpotent-A controls (A^2=0, including the n013 matrix) give det JF a nonzero constant (F is Keller) -- evidence that nilpotency of A is forced by the Keller condition on this reduced family, not merely a sufficient choice.

source.title: For F = X + (AX)^{*3}: C^2 -> C^2, in a finite sweep of 2x2 matrices A, every tested non-nilpotent A (identity, diag(2,0), upper-unipotent, rank-1-trace-2) yields det JF that is NOT a nonzero constant (F fails the Keller condition), while the tested nilpotent controls (A^2=0) yield det JF a nonzero constant (F is Keller) -- consistent with nilpotency of A being forced by, not merely sufficient for, the Keller condition on this reduced family.

titles.text: chk-l1-05: nilpotency forced by Keller condition in cubic-linear family

phrasings.text: Verified: F=(x1+x2^3,x2+x3^3,x3+x4^3,x4) over C^4 (JH nilpotent index exactly 4) is Keller; the explicit triangular back-substitution G is a two-sided polynomial inverse of degree 27, exactly saturating (deg F)^(n-1)=3^3=27 -- extending n033's n=3 saturating-termination pattern one nilpotency-index level higher.

source.title: For the n=4 shift-nilpotent cubic-linear map F=(x1+x2^3, x2+x3^3, x3+x4^3, x4) over C^4 (JH nilpotent of index exactly 4), F is Keller (det JF=1 identically), the explicit triangular back-substitution map G is an exact two-sided polynomial inverse, and deg(G) = 27 exactly saturates the (deg F)^(n-1) = 3^3 = 27 inverse-degree bound -- extending the n=3 saturating-termination pattern (chk-l3-02/n033) one nilpotency-index level higher.

titles.text: chk-l1-06: n=4 nilpotency-index-4 termination saturates the degree bound

phrasings.text: If F: C^n -> C^n is a non-injective Keller map for some n >= 1, then deg F >= 3: by Wang (1980, n003), every Keller map of degree <= 2 is a polynomial automorphism, hence injective.

titles.text: Counterexample degree floor (general n, Wang)

phrasings.text: If F: C^2 -> C^2 is a non-injective Keller map, then deg F > 100: by Moh (1983, n006), every Keller map C^2 -> C^2 of degree <= 100 is a polynomial automorphism, hence injective.

titles.text: Counterexample degree floor for n=2 (Moh)

phrasings.text: If a non-injective Keller map exists for some n, then there exists m >= n and a non-injective Keller map of the reduced form G = X+H: C^m -> C^m with H cubic homogeneous and JH nilpotent (via n004), and in fact of the Druzkowski cubic-linear form G=X+(AX)^{*3} for nilpotent A (via n005) -- the BCW/Yagzhev and Druzkowski reductions are constructive, so a hypothetical counterexample transfers into this reduced family rather than merely being constrained by it in the abstract.

titles.text: A counterexample transfers into the cubic-homogeneous/cubic-linear reduced form

phrasings.text: If F=X+(AX)^{*3}: C^m -> C^m is a Keller map, then A cannot be drawn freely from non-nilpotent matrices: chk-l1-05 shows every tested non-nilpotent A fails the Keller condition, so a hypothetical counterexample confined to this reduced family still cannot escape the nilpotency constraint on A.

titles.text: Nilpotency is forced, not a free choice, in the cubic-linear reduced form

phrasings.text: If F=X+H: C^n -> C^n (H cubic homogeneous, JH nilpotent) is a non-injective Keller map, then by n010's termination-equivalence the tree-expansion formal inverse of F does not terminate at any finite degree, despite JH having finite nilpotency index -- a qualitatively different behavior from every checked instance (n033, chk-l1-06), where finite nilpotency index has always come with exact termination saturating the (deg F)^(n-1) bound.

titles.text: A counterexample forces the tree-expansion inverse to never terminate

phrasings.text: For a Keller map F=(P,Q): C^2 -> C^2 with leading forms P_p=c1*h^(p/d), Q_q=c2*h^(q/d) (h a homogeneous binary form of degree d=gcd(p,q), per the n040 characterization), the base points of F's projective extension on the line at infinity Z=0 (n043) are exactly the distinct roots of h in P^1: F has exactly ONE base point at infinity if and only if h has a single distinct linear factor (h = c*l^d for one linear form l, i.e. h is a perfect power), and exactly d DISTINCT simple base points if and only if h is squarefree.

titles.text: Base-point count at infinity = number of distinct linear factors of the leading-form gcd h

phrasings.text: Verified: for h1=(x+y)^2 (n047's leading-form gcd, gcd(2,6)=2) sympy factorization finds exactly 1 distinct linear factor -> 1 base point at infinity; for h2=(x-y)(x+2y) (n048/n049's gcd, gcd(4,6)=2) factorization finds exactly 2 distinct linear factors -> 2 distinct base points, checked by exact factorization.

source.title: For leading-form gcd h1=(x+y)^2 (n047, gcd(2,6)=2) the base locus of the Keller map's projective extension on the line at infinity is a SINGLE point (h1 has 1 distinct linear factor, with multiplicity 2); for h2=(x-y)(x+2y) (n048/n049, gcd(4,6)=2) the base locus is 2 DISTINCT simple points (h2 is squarefree with 2 distinct linear factors) -- i.e. the number of distinct base points at infinity equals the number of distinct linear factors of the shared leading-form gcd h, checked by exact factorization.

titles.text: chk-l2-05: base-point count matches distinct-linear-factor count of h in two worked cases

demoted.reason: Refuted by chk-l2-12/chk-l2-11: n063's claim that F=(x+y^2,y)'s base point resolves after exactly one blow-up with the exceptional divisor fully contracted to a point is contradicted by explicit root-following -- 3 blow-ups are needed, and the locus never contracts. See n161.

phrasings.text: For the Keller polynomial automorphism F=(x+y^2,y): C^2 -> C^2 (leading-form gcd h=y, a single linear form, per n061's single-base-point case), the unique base point [1:0:0] of F's projective extension on the line at infinity resolves after exactly one blow-up, and the resulting exceptional divisor is contracted by the resolved map to the single point [1:0:0] rather than mapping dominantly onto a curve.

titles.text: Automorphism baseline: single base point resolves in one blow-up, exceptional divisor contracts to a point

demoted.reason: Refuted by chk-l2-12: the check's own chart-A computation (reproduced exactly) shows a residual indeterminate point on the exceptional divisor after the first blow-up that its chart-B check never actually resolved (chart B covers a different point of the same P^1). Full resolution needs 3 blow-ups, and the locus does not contract. See n161, chk-l2-12.

phrasings.text: Verified: for F=(x+y^2,y) (det JF=1), the projective extension's unique base point [1:0:0] resolves after exactly one blow-up in both charts, and on the exceptional divisor (u=0) the resolved map value is the constant [1:0:0] for every v, i.e. the exceptional divisor is entirely contracted to that single point -- checked by explicit symbolic computation in both blow-up charts.

source.title: For the Keller automorphism F=(x+y^2,y) (det JF=1), the projective extension to P^2 has a single base point [1:0:0] on the line at infinity (matching the single-root leading-form gcd h=y); blowing up that point resolves the indeterminacy after exactly one blow-up, and the entire exceptional divisor is contracted by the resolved map to the single point [1:0:0] (not mapped dominantly onto a curve), checked by explicit symbolic computation in both blow-up charts.

titles.text: chk-l2-06: blow-up of F=(x+y^2,y)'s base point resolves in one step, exceptional divisor contracts

comments.text: Sunk (round 3): n065 rested on the unverified leap that non-properness (n045) forces non-contraction of a resolved exceptional divisor, calibrated against n063's degree-2 automorphism baseline where contraction holds. chk-l2-07 (a second, deeper blow-up-chart computation on a real degree-3 Keller automorphism, n106/n107) shows that baseline itself breaks by degree 3 -- contraction already fails for a genuine automorphism, so its absence cannot certify non-injectivity. See n108 for the honest replacement conditional.

demoted.reason: The 'contraction' baseline it leaned on (n063/n064) turns out to be degree-2-specific: chk-l2-07 shows a genuine degree-3 Keller AUTOMORPHISM already fails to fully contract its resolved base point (n107). Since n065's inference contrasted a hypothetical non-automorphism's non-contraction against an automorphism baseline that itself doesn't hold at higher degree, the inference no longer distinguishes anything. Superseded by n108 (sharper, honest downgrade).

phrasings.text: If F=(P,Q): C^2 -> C^2 is a Keller map that is not a polynomial automorphism (n045's hypothetical JC counterexample) and has a single base point at infinity (n061's h-is-a-perfect-power case), then the exceptional divisor produced by resolving that base point cannot be entirely contracted to one point by F's extension the way it is in the verified automorphism baseline (n063/n064): some component of the resolved indeterminacy locus must carry a positive-dimensional (non-constant) image, since F's failure to be proper (n045) means points escaping to infinity along that base point's direction cannot all be swept to a single image point without violating non-properness somewhere along the blow-up tower.

titles.text: Conditional: a non-automorphism Keller map's single base point cannot fully contract under resolution

comments.text: Sunk (round 3), honoring f10. n066's claim that AMS 'bears on' 2D Keller-map geometry at infinity overstated a real but non-evidential relation: AMS's single-place technique and the single-base-point regime (n061) are structurally analogous problems, not ones where one supplies a premise for the other. Left un-replaced rather than restating the same analogy as a new node -- the honest content of n066 IS the friction text at f10, and duplicating it as a claim node would not upgrade its epistemic status.

demoted.reason: Per f10 (self-flagged friction, round 2): the n046-to-n066 grounding was a shared-technique ANALOGY (both are single-point Newton-Puiseux/approximate-root analyses at infinity), not logical support -- n046 doesn't supply a premise n066 needs, it supplies a methodological precedent. Sinking rather than dressing the analogy up as evidential grounding; no verified computation can repair an analogy into a logical dependency.

phrasings.text: The Abhyankar-Moh-Suzuki embedding theorem (n046) bears on 2D Keller-map geometry at infinity specifically in the single-base-point regime (n061): an embedded line's projective closure meets the line at infinity in exactly one point, and AMS's proof analyzes that single point via Newton-Puiseux/approximate-root expansion; a Keller map whose leading-form gcd h is a perfect power of one linear form likewise has exactly one base point at infinity (n061), so the same single-point local-expansion technique -- not a coincidence of terminology -- is the natural tool there. When h is squarefree with multiple distinct base points (n061's other case), the points decouple and no single-point AMS-style analysis is forced.

titles.text: AMS's single-place-at-infinity technique is the natural tool exactly in the single-base-point regime

phrasings.text: Over a field k of characteristic 0 and for any n >= 1, the kernel of the partial derivative operator d/dx_i on k[x_1,...,x_n] equals exactly the subring of polynomials not involving x_i, k[x_1,...,x_(i-1),x_(i+1),...,x_n] -- which for n >= 2 properly contains non-constant elements (e.g. any x_j with j != i lies in ker(d/dx_i) while being non-constant), so 'ker(d/dx_i) is exactly the constants k' is false as a claim about the full n-variable ring once n >= 2. It is exactly true only when restricted to the univariate subring k[x_i] alone (no other variables): there, a polynomial in x_i alone with identically-vanishing derivative must have degree 0. This corrects and replaces n025's over-general 'kernel = constants k for every i' claim, which conflated the univariate fact with the full n-variable ring.

titles.text: Precise char-0 kernel: constants only on the univariate slice

phrasings.text: For every 2x2 matrix A over C, A^2=0 implies det(A)=0 identically (equivalently rank(A) <= 1); consequently there is no 'rank-2 nilpotent 2x2' Druzkowski instance to examine, and the round-1 rank<=1 exhaustion (n036/n037, 32 matrices) already covers every nonzero nilpotent case in n=2.

titles.text: No rank-2 nilpotent 2x2 matrix exists over C

phrasings.text: Verified: for a general 2x2 matrix A over C, solving A^2=0 symbolically gives exactly two solution branches (A=0, and a rank-1 family b=-a^2/c,d=-a); det(A) is identically 0 on every branch, so no rank-2 (invertible) nilpotent 2x2 matrix exists.

source.title: For every 2x2 matrix A over C with A^2=0, det(A)=0 identically (so rank(A) <= 1); in particular no rank-2 (invertible) nilpotent 2x2 matrix exists, so the round-1 rank<=1 family (n036/n037) already covers every nonzero nilpotent 2x2 case.

titles.text: chk-l4-06: A^2=0 forces det(A)=0 for 2x2 A (no rank-2 nilpotent)

phrasings.text: Over a field k of characteristic p > 0 and for any n >= 1, ker(d/dx_i) on k[x_1,...,x_n] equals k[x_1,...,x_(i-1),x_(i+1),...,x_n][x_i^p] -- adjoin the p-th power x_i^p to the subring of polynomials not involving x_i -- strictly larger than the char-0 kernel by exactly that p-th-power piece. This is strictly larger than 'the subring generated by x_i^p alone' (no dependence on the other n-1 variables): e.g. n=2, i=1, p=5, both x_2^3 and x_1^5*x_2^2 lie in ker(d/dx_1) despite lying outside the subring generated by x_1^5. Corrects n024's imprecise description of the char-p kernel.

titles.text: Precise char-p kernel: adjoin x_i^p to the other-variable subring

phrasings.text: Leading-form nondegeneracy at infinity (the finite-morphism criterion): if F=(F_1,...,F_n): C^n -> C^n is a polynomial map of degree d and the top-degree-d homogeneous parts F_1^{(d)},...,F_n^{(d)} (taken to be 0 if deg F_i < d) have {0} as their only common zero in C^n, then F is a finite morphism of affine space and hence proper; for a Keller map with this property, properness (n044) already forces it to be a polynomial automorphism.

titles.text: Leading-form nondegeneracy at infinity: a checkable sufficient properness criterion

phrasings.text: Verified over QQ: for every degree d in 0..12, a univariate polynomial a0+a1*x+...+ad*x^d has identically-vanishing d/dx only when a1=...=ad=0, i.e. ker(d/dx) restricted to QQ[x] alone is exactly the constants QQ (exhaustive to degree 12). This supports n067's precise restatement and, since it only ever concerns the single-variable slice, it also shows n025's unrestricted 'ker(d/dx_i) = constants k for every n' is not what this computation establishes -- n025's broader claim is false for n>=2 (e.g. x_2 in ker(d/dx_1) on k[x_1,x_2] is non-constant), refuted by the trivial multivariate containment noted in n067.

source.title: Over QQ (characteristic 0), for every degree d in 0..12, a univariate polynomial a0 + a1*x + ... + ad*x^d in the single variable x has identically-vanishing derivative d/dx only when a1=...=ad=0 -- i.e. ker(d/dx) restricted to QQ[x] alone is exactly the constants QQ, exhaustively checked up to degree 12 (the mechanism relies on i != 0 in QQ for i=1..d, which is exactly where characteristic p=i would break it).

titles.text: chk-l5-04: char-0 univariate kernel triviality, deg<=12

phrasings.text: For every n and every rank-1 nilpotent A = u v^T over C^n (v.u=0, u,v != 0), the Druzkowski map F=X+(AX)^{*3}: C^n -> C^n has det(JF) = 1 + 3*(v.x)^2*(v.u^{*3}) identically, where v.u^{*3} := sum_i v_i*u_i^3 (a consequence of the rank-1 matrix-determinant lemma det(I+ab^T)=1+b.a applied to JF=I+3(v.x)^2 u^{*3} v^T); hence F is Keller iff v.u^{*3}=0, in which case det(JF)=1 exactly. Verified by direct symbolic computation for n=2 (n052/n053, specializing to a*c*(a-b)*(a+b)=0) and n=3 (chk-l4-07); the general-n>=4 case rests on the same determinant-lemma argument but has not itself been symbolically checked.

For every n and every rank-1 nilpotent A = u v^T over C^n (v.u=0, u,v != 0), the Druzkowski map F=X+(AX)^{*3}: C^n -> C^n has det(JF) = 1 + 3*(v.x)^2*(v.u^{*3}) identically, where v.u^{*3} := sum_i v_i*u_i^3 (a consequence of the rank-1 matrix-determinant lemma det(I+ab^T)=1+b.a applied to JF=I+3(v.x)^2 u^{*3} v^T); hence F is Keller iff v.u^{*3}=0, in which case det(JF)=1 exactly. Verified by direct symbolic computation for n=2 (n052/n053), n=3 (chk-l4-07), and n=4 (chk-l4-09, concrete generic u); n=5 was attempted with the same method but exceeded the practical time budget (untested, not merely unattempted-in-principle).

titles.text: Generalized closed-form Keller criterion for rank-1 nilpotent A (conjectured all n)

phrasings.text: For every nonlinear Keller map F=(F_1,F_2): C^2 -> C^2 (deg F >= 2), the leading-form-nondegenerate-at-origin criterion (the node stating: top-degree parts share only the trivial common zero) never applies: since deg F_1, deg F_2 >= 1 are forced by the Keller condition, deg F_1 + deg F_2 > 2 whenever deg F >= 2, so the top-degree parts P,Q satisfy J(P,Q)=0; a homogeneous binary form of positive degree always has a nonzero root direction, so P and Q always share a nontrivial common zero beyond the origin. The criterion is structurally vacuous for n=2.

titles.text: The leading-form criterion is structurally vacuous for n=2 nonlinear Keller maps

phrasings.text: Verified over F_5: ker(d/dx_1) on F_5[x_1,x_2] contains x_2^3 and x_1^5*x_2^2 (both outside the subring generated by x_1^5 alone, since they involve x_2 non-trivially) as well as x_1^5, while x_1 itself is not in the kernel -- so ker(d/dx_1) = F_5[x_2][x_1^5], strictly larger than 'subring generated by x_1^5 alone'. This directly refutes n024's claim that the char-p kernel 'equals the subring generated by x_i^p': the exhibited elements x_2^3 and x_1^5*x_2^2 are kernel members outside that subring. Supports n070's corrected statement instead.

source.title: Over F_5, ker(d/dx1) on F_5[x1,x2] contains x2^3 and x1^5*x2^2 (both outside the subring generated by x1^5 alone, since they involve x2 non-trivially) as well as x1^5, while x1 itself is NOT in the kernel -- so ker(d/dx1) = F_5[x2][x1^5], strictly larger than 'subring generated by x1^5 alone'.

titles.text: chk-l5-05 [refutes n024]: kernel exceeds subring gen by x_i^p

phrasings.text: Verified: for A=u v^T over C^3 with v.u=0 (rank-1 nilpotent), det(JF) = 1 + 3*(v.x)^2*(v.u^{*3}) identically (symbolic difference reduces to 0), so F=X+(AX)^{*3} is Keller iff v.u^{*3}=0; the n=3 formula specializes to n052's n=2 criterion a*c*(a-b)*(a+b)=0 exactly.

source.title: For rank-1 nilpotent A=u*v^T over C^3 (v.u=0), the Druzkowski map F=X+(AX)^{*3}: C^3 -> C^3 has det(JF) = 1 + 3*(v.x)^2*(v.u^{*3}) identically (v.u^{*3} := sum_i v_i*u_i^3), hence F is Keller iff v.u^{*3}=0 (giving det(JF)=1 exactly), generalizing n052/n053's n=2 criterion a*c*(a-b)*(a+b)=0 as the n=2 case of the same v.u^{*3}=0 pattern.

titles.text: chk-l4-07: rank-1 Keller criterion verified for n=3, matches n=2 case

phrasings.text: For the entire cubic-linear (Druzkowski) reduced family F = X + (AX)^{*3}, A an n x n nilpotent matrix, the leading-form-nondegenerate-at-origin criterion is also always vacuous, by a different mechanism than the n=2 case: the degree-3 leading part is H_i=(Ax)_i^3, whose common zero locus equals ker(A) exactly; every nilpotent matrix is singular, so ker(A) is nontrivial (dim >= 1) for every A in the family, for every n.

titles.text: The leading-form criterion is also vacuous on the entire cubic-linear reduced family

phrasings.text: The leading-form criterion's vacuity on both known reduced families is not evidence against properness: at the hyperplane at infinity x0=0, the full homogenization of F reduces exactly to the leading-form system (all lower-degree terms carry positive powers of x0), so a naive 'use the full homogenization instead' fix collapses back to the same vacuous test. A properness criterion for these families must instead track how the map resolves the shared base locus under blow-up, not merely test non-degeneracy at the origin; formalizing a checkable version of that resolved criterion is open.

titles.text: Vacuity is a limitation of the naive test, not evidence against properness

comments.text: Contrast with n036 (index-2 rank-1 family): there, 24/32 nilpotent instances -- most of them genuinely non-triangular -- were Keller. At index 3, this small exhaustion found zero non-triangular Keller instances; the sample is only 6 matrices (not a full exhaustion of index-3 conjugates), so this is suggestive, not conclusive, that non-triangularity gets harder to sustain as nilpotency index rises.

phrasings.text: Within an explicit 6-matrix family of invertible-integer conjugates A=M J M^{-1} of the standard nilpotency-index-3 Jordan block J (3x3), giving Druzkowski maps F=X+(AX)^{*3}: C^3 -> C^3, exactly the 3 conjugates under which A stays triangular up to a coordinate permutation are Keller (det JF=1 identically); the 3 genuinely non-triangular conjugates all fail to be Keller (det JF non-constant). No non-triangular index-3 Keller instance was found within this bounded family.

titles.text: At nilpotency index 3, tested Druzkowski Keller instances are all triangular up to relabeling

phrasings.text: Verified: among 6 explicit invertible-integer conjugates A=M J M^{-1} of the index-3 Jordan block J (3x3), the Druzkowski maps F=X+(AX)^{*3} are Keller (det JF=1 identically) exactly for the 3 conjugates preserving triangularity up to coordinate permutation (M=I, upper-triangular unipotent, coordinate-swap); the other 3 non-triangular conjugates all have non-constant det JF.

source.title: Among 6 explicit invertible-integer conjugates A=M*J*M^{-1} of the standard nilpotency-index-3 Jordan block J (3x3, over Z, listed in the transcript) giving Druzkowski maps F=X+(AX)^{*3}: C^3 -> C^3, exactly the 3 conjugates that preserve triangularity up to coordinate permutation (M=I, upper-triangular unipotent, and the coordinate-swap permutation) are Keller (det JF=1 identically); the other 3 genuinely non-triangular conjugates all have non-constant det JF (not Keller).

titles.text: chk-l4-08: 3/6 index-3 conjugates Keller, all triangular; 3/6 non-triangular fail

phrasings.text: Verified: for F=X+(AX)^{*3} with A=[[1,1],[-1,-1]] (n=2) and A=Jordan nilpotent block [[0,1,0],[0,0,1],[0,0,0]] (n=3), the degree-3 leading forms H_i=(Ax)_i^3 vanish exactly on ker(A); an explicit nonzero witness vector in ker(A) kills all H_i in both cases, confirming the leading-form criterion never applies to this reduced family.

source.title: For the cubic-linear reduced form F = X + (AX)^{*3} with A a nilpotent n x n matrix (n=2: A=[[1,1],[-1,-1]], the chk-l1-02 matrix; n=3: the nilpotent Jordan block [[0,1,0],[0,0,1],[0,0,0]]), the degree-3 leading part of F is H_i=(Ax)_i^3, whose common zero locus in C^n equals ker(A) exactly; since every nilpotent matrix is singular, ker(A) is always nontrivial (dimension >= 1, confirmed by an explicit nonzero witness vector), so the leading-form-nondegenerate-at-origin properness criterion never applies to any map in this entire reduced family, for any n.

titles.text: chk-l3-05: cubic-linear family leading forms vanish exactly on ker(A), always nontrivial

phrasings.text: Verified: for the chk-l1-02 Keller map F1=x1+(x1+x2)^3, F2=x2-(x1+x2)^3, the top-degree parts P=(x1+x2)^3, Q=-(x1+x2)^3 have J(P,Q)=0 identically and share the nontrivial common zero (1,-1) (the full line x1+x2=0); the leading-form-nondegenerate-at-origin criterion cannot certify this map, even though it is independently known proper via its explicit inverse.

source.title: For the verified Keller map F1=x1+(x1+x2)^3, F2=x2-(x1+x2)^3 (chk-l1-02), the top-degree (degree-3) homogeneous parts P=(x1+x2)^3, Q=-(x1+x2)^3 have J(P,Q)=0 identically and share a nontrivial common zero at (x1,x2)=(1,-1) (in fact along the full line x1+x2=0), so the leading-form-nondegenerate-at-origin sufficient criterion for properness does not apply to (cannot certify) this explicit Keller map, despite the map being independently known proper.

titles.text: chk-l3-04: known-proper cubic-linear map's leading forms share a nontrivial common zero

phrasings.text: Verified by symbolic identity (generic coefficients, 3 degree pairs (p,q) in {(2,3),(2,6),(3,4)}): the top-total-degree homogeneous part of J(F1,F2) equals J(P,Q) for the top-degree parts P,Q exactly; since det JF constant forces this top part to vanish, J(P,Q)=0 is forced whenever p+q>2, strengthening the general law with a multi-case identity beyond the single chk-l2-01 instance.

source.title: For symbolic homogeneous top-degree parts P (deg p), Q (deg q) of two polynomials in x,y, plus fully generic lower-total-degree perturbations, the top-total-degree (degree p+q-2) homogeneous component of the Jacobian J(F1,F2) equals J(P,Q) exactly as a polynomial identity in all coefficient symbols, checked for (p,q) in {(2,3),(2,6),(3,4)}; consequently, since det(JF) constant forces every positive-degree homogeneous part of J(F1,F2) to vanish, J(P,Q)=0 is a forced consequence of the Keller condition whenever p+q>2, for any n=2 polynomial map with those leading degrees.

titles.text: chk-l3-03: multi-case symbolic identity forcing J(top,top)=0 for n=2 Keller maps

phrasings.text: If H=(H_1,...,H_n) has every component homogeneous of degree exactly 3 and JH is identically the zero matrix, then over any field where 3 is invertible (in particular char 0) H is identically 0 -- by Euler's identity sum_j x_j*dH_i/dx_j = 3*H_i, JH=0 forces 3*H_i=0 hence H_i=0. This is the precise char-0 (more precisely char != 3) ingredient blocking a Frobenius-style derivative-blind cubic perturbation inside the reduced cubic-homogeneous family (F=X+H, H cubic homogeneous, JH nilpotent) at the JH=0 sub-case: unlike the general single-variable mechanism (n067/n070), this is Euler's identity plus invertibility of the degree, and it fails exactly when char divides deg H=3.

titles.text: Euler's identity is the char-0 blocker for the reduced family's JH=0 case

phrasings.text: Over F_3 -- the one characteristic where n084's Euler-identity blocker fails, since 3=0 there -- the diagonal cubic-homogeneous map H=(x_1^3,...,x_n^3) has JH identically the zero matrix, so F=X-H is a genuine reduced-family instance (JH nilpotent, in fact zero) whose Keller condition det JF=1 is automatic -- yet F is non-injective (F(0,..)=F(1,0,..,0), since 0-0^3=1-1^3=0 in F_3). A reduced-family char-p collision that needs characteristic equal to the family's own homogeneity degree (3) exactly, not merely 'some prime', sharpening n031 for the reduced-family case.

titles.text: Reduced-family char-p collision needs char = deg H exactly

phrasings.text: Verified over QQ: for H1 homogeneous of degree 3 in (x1,x2), Euler's identity x1*dH1/dx1+x2*dH1/dx2=3*H1 holds identically, and JH1=0 (all first partials vanish) forces 3*H1=0 hence H1=0 (3 invertible in QQ) -- checked exhaustively over the full 4-dimensional space of degree-3 homogeneous polynomials in 2 variables (fully generic symbolic H1, solving the vanishing-partials linear system). Supports n084.

source.title: Over QQ, for H1 homogeneous of degree 3 in (x1,x2), Euler's identity x1*dH1/dx1 + x2*dH1/dx2 = 3*H1 holds identically, and JH1 (all first partials) == 0 forces 3*H1 == 0 hence H1 == 0 (since 3 is invertible in QQ) -- verified exhaustively over the full 4-dimensional space of degree-3 homogeneous polynomials in 2 variables (a fully generic symbolic H1).

titles.text: chk-l5-06: Euler-identity rigidity, exhaustive over cubic forms in 2 vars

phrasings.text: Verified over F_3: the cubic-homogeneous diagonal map H=(x1^3,x2^3) has JH identically zero mod 3, so F=X-H has det JF=1 identically (Keller condition automatic from JH=0) -- yet F(0,0)=F(1,0), an explicit non-injectivity collision at char 3, the reduced cubic-homogeneous family's own degree. Supports n085.

source.title: Over F_3, the cubic-homogeneous diagonal map H=(x1^3,x2^3) has Jacobian matrix JH identically zero (mod 3), so F=X-H has det JF == 1 identically (Keller condition automatic from nilpotent/zero JH, no extra work) -- yet F(0,0) = F(1,0), an explicit non-injectivity collision at char 3, the reduced cubic-homogeneous family's own degree.

titles.text: chk-l5-07: F_3 diagonal-cubic reduced-family collision

phrasings.text: Verified: for F=(x1+x2^3,x2+x3^3,x3) over C^3 (JH nilpotent index 3), the Picard iteration G_0=Y, G_{k+1}=Y-H(G_k) computing the formal-inverse fixed point G=Y-H(G) has degree sequence deg(G_k)=1,3,9,9 for k=0,1,2,3, stabilizes exactly (G_3=G_4 as polynomials, not merely to some truncation order) at k=3, and G_3 is F's exact two-sided polynomial inverse of degree 9=(deg F)^(n-1)=3^2.

source.title: For F=(x1+x2^3, x2+x3^3, x3) over C^3 (JH nilpotent index 3), the Picard iteration G_0=Y, G_{k+1}=Y-H(G_k) computing the formal-inverse fixed point G=Y-H(G) has degree sequence deg(G_k) = 1, 3, 9, 9 for k=0,1,2,3, stabilizes exactly (G_3 = G_4 as polynomials, not merely to some truncation order) at k=3, and the stabilized G_3 is verified to be F's exact two-sided polynomial inverse of degree 9 = (deg F)^(n-1) = 3^2 -- i.e. the iteration itself reaches a fixed point in finitely many steps (equal to the nilpotency index) rather than merely producing a polynomial of bounded degree by some other means.

titles.text: chk-l1-07: n=3 Picard truncations stabilize at k=3, degree 1,3,9,9

phrasings.text: Verified: for F=(x1+x2^3,x2+x3^3,x3+x4^3,x4) over C^4 (JH nilpotent index 4), the Picard iteration G_0=Y, G_{k+1}=Y-H(G_k) computing the formal-inverse fixed point G=Y-H(G) has degree sequence deg(G_k)=1,3,9,27,27 for k=0,1,2,3,4, stabilizes exactly (G_4=G_5 as polynomials) at k=4, and G_4 is F's exact two-sided polynomial inverse of degree 27=(deg F)^(n-1)=3^3.

source.title: For F=(x1+x2^3, x2+x3^3, x3+x4^3, x4) over C^4 (JH nilpotent index 4), the Picard iteration G_0=Y, G_{k+1}=Y-H(G_k) computing the formal-inverse fixed point G=Y-H(G) has degree sequence deg(G_k) = 1, 3, 9, 27, 27 for k=0,1,2,3,4, stabilizes exactly (G_4 = G_5 as polynomials) at k=4, and the stabilized G_4 is verified to be F's exact two-sided polynomial inverse of degree 27 = (deg F)^(n-1) = 3^3 -- one more tripling step than the n=3 case (chk-l1-07), with the iteration count and final degree both advancing in lockstep with the nilpotency index.

titles.text: chk-l1-08: n=4 Picard truncations stabilize at k=4, degree 1,3,9,27,27

phrasings.text: For the length-p shift-nilpotent chain family F=X+H on C^p (F_i=x_i+x_{i+1}^3, JH nilpotent of index exactly p), what terminates is the Picard iteration G_{k+1}=Y-H(G_k) (G_0=Y) computing the tree-expansion formal inverse: deg(G_k) grows as exactly 3^k for k=0,...,p-1 and then plateaus (G_p=G_{p-1}), so the iteration reaches its fixed point in exactly p steps and the terminal degree exactly saturates (deg F)^(p-1), for p=2 (n039), p=3, and p=4.

titles.text: What terminates: the Picard iteration itself, in p steps, at degree 3^(p-1)

phrasings.text: For F=X+H: C^n -> C^n (H cubic homogeneous, JH nilpotent), the tree-expansion formal inverse terminates at finite degree if and only if the Picard truncations G_k (G_0=Y, G_{k+1}=Y-H(G_k)) of the defining fixed-point equation G=Y-H(G) stabilize exactly (G_k=G_{k+1} as polynomials) at some finite k; if it fails to terminate, deg(G_k) must increase strictly for every k, with no finite plateau ever occurring, rather than the bounded tripling-then-plateau growth (1,3,...,3^{p-1},3^{p-1}) seen in every checked instance.

titles.text: Sharp signature: termination = Picard-truncation degree plateau; non-termination = unbounded strict degree growth

phrasings.text: If F=X+H: C^n -> C^n (H cubic homogeneous, JH nilpotent) is a Keller map whose tree-expansion formal inverse does not terminate at any finite degree, then the Picard truncations G_k of G=Y-H(G) fail to stabilize at any finite k -- deg(G_k) must increase strictly for every k, with no finite plateau -- unlike every verified Keller instance checked (n039, chk-l1-07, chk-l1-08), where deg(G_k) reaches a fixed plateau within k = nilpotency-index steps.

titles.text: The non-termination signature: unbounded strict Picard-degree growth, never a plateau

phrasings.text: If M, N are connected, locally compact Hausdorff spaces and F: M -> N is a local homeomorphism (in particular a local diffeomorphism or local biholomorphism) that is also proper (preimages of compact sets are compact), then F is a covering map onto N; this is the precise topological fact doing the work in the properness-to-covering step, and it fails as soon as either hypothesis (properness or local-homeomorphism) is dropped.

titles.text: Proper local homeomorphism implies covering map

phrasings.text: If p: M -> N is a covering map with N simply connected and locally path-connected and M connected, then p is a homeomorphism: N simply connected forces every covering of N to be trivial (a disjoint union of copies of N), and connectedness of M forces that trivial covering to have exactly one sheet, so p is a bijective local homeomorphism.

titles.text: Covering of a simply-connected connected base is a homeomorphism

phrasings.text: If F: C^n -> C^n is a polynomial map that is bijective as a map of sets, then F^{-1} is itself given by polynomials (not merely continuous or holomorphic): properness plus quasi-finiteness of F makes it a finite morphism of varieties, bijectivity makes it birational (degree 1), and a finite birational morphism onto a normal variety (C^n is smooth, hence normal) is an isomorphism of varieties by Zariski's Main Theorem, so the inverse is regular, i.e. polynomial.

titles.text: Bijective polynomial self-map of C^n has polynomial inverse (Zariski's Main Theorem step)

phrasings.text: Verified: F(x,y)=(x+y^3,y) on C^2 is Keller (det JF=1), has explicit polynomial inverse G(x,y)=(x-y^3,y) checked exactly by composition both ways, and over 6 sample target points the fiber F^{-1}(a,b) has cardinality exactly 1 every time -- the constant-fiber-size signature of a trivial 1-sheeted covering map.

source.title: The triangular polynomial map F(x,y)=(x+y^3, y) on C^2 is Keller (det JF = 1, a nonzero constant), has the explicit polynomial inverse G(x,y)=(x-y^3, y) verified exactly by composition (F.G = id, G.F = id), and over 6 sample target points in C^2 the fiber F^{-1}(a,b) is computed by exact symbolic solve to have cardinality exactly 1 every time, matching G(a,b) -- the constant-fiber-size signature of a (trivial, 1-sheeted) covering map.

titles.text: chk-l3-06: triangular Keller map, explicit polynomial inverse, constant fiber size 1

phrasings.text: Verified: F(x,y)=(e^x cos y, e^x sin y) on R^2 is a local diffeomorphism everywhere (det JF=e^{2x} != 0) but not proper; its fiber F^{-1}(w) has cardinality 0 at w=(0,0) (unreachable) and >=3 explicit distinct preimages at w=(1,0) -- a non-constant fiber size, so F fails the covering-map signature exactly where properness fails.

source.title: For the everywhere-local-diffeomorphism map F(x,y)=(e^x cos y, e^x sin y) on R^2 (det JF = e^{2x} != 0 everywhere, but F is not proper), the fiber F^{-1}(w) has cardinality 0 at w=(0,0) (no real x solves e^x=0) and cardinality >= 3 (exhibiting 3 distinct explicit preimages (0,2*pi*k), k=0,1,2) at w=(1,0) -- a non-constant fiber size, so F fails the covering-map signature exactly where properness fails, contrasting the constant-fiber covering instance of chk-l3-06.

titles.text: chk-l3-07: everywhere-local-diffeomorphism, non-proper map has non-constant fiber size

phrasings.text: In the n016/n017-to-n044 covering-space route to JC, every constituent step (Keller implies local biholomorphism; proper local homeomorphism implies covering map; covering of a simply-connected base implies homeomorphism; bijective polynomial implies polynomial inverse via Zariski's Main Theorem) is an unconditionally true, machine-checkable classical theorem once its stated hypotheses hold -- the single hypothesis that cannot be established for a general Keller map, and on which the whole chain hinges, is properness itself; since n044/n020 already show 'F is proper' is equivalent to JC holding for F, this route supplies zero independent leverage on the conjecture and instead pinpoints exactly where the difficulty is concentrated.

titles.text: Properness is the sole unverifiable hypothesis in the covering-space route to JC
